extracts.extract: The first thing to do is to make sure you understand what you're looking at. What does each row represent? Are there any columns you don't understand? Do you have all the data you need? Is there anything missing that would be useful for our analysis?
extracts.relevance_rationale: Understanding what each row and column represents before analysis is a fundamental data literacy practice

extracts.extract: Then you want to come up with the questions you want to ask. We're going into an "interview," so it's a good idea to have a list of questions at the ready. But just like an interview with a human, it's okay to veer off your list at some point later.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Preparing interview questions for data and remaining flexible is an enduring analytical thinking practice

extracts.extract: General rule of thumb is that you'll want to use percent change (rather than simple subtraction) in any situation where the things you are comparing (city budgets, county populations, city crime totals, state spending, school enrollments) have a lot of underlying variability like we have here.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Knowing when to use percent change vs simple subtraction is enduring statistical reasoning

extracts.extract: The data didn't come with a total, but even if it did, I'd recommend calculating your own total just to check their work. (I've seen front page stories where news organizations discovered errors in city government totals).
extracts.relevance_rationale: Independently calculating totals to check supplied figures is an enduring verification practice

extracts.extract: The rule of thumb in using average versus median is that you should use median (which returns the middle value) in situations where you have big outliers -- either a few values that are really low or really high compared to the majority of the other values.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Understanding when to use median vs mean based on outlier presence is enduring statistical literacy

extracts.extract: Make sure the columns are labeled and that the column label only takes up one row. Any notes or titles --or anything else that might be above or below the data table -- should be separated from the data by a blank row (or more). No empty rows or empty columns within the area where the data is located.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Spreadsheet hygiene rules endure but should mention that LLMs can now parse messy data structures that previously required manual cleanup

extracts.extract: Pivot Tables, which are available in Microsoft Excel and Google Sheets, are the most powerful tool (in my opinion) you have in a spreadsheet program. It allows you to take very detailed data -- in this case, where one row represents a reported crime -- and generate the summary findings that you need for your story.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Pivot tables remain useful but LLMs can now generate equivalent aggregations via code or natural language queries, broadening access

extracts.extract: Notice the wide variety of ways that Hispanic and Non-Hispanic are listed? In order to get a good count, we need to make them consistent. This is called standardizing data.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Data standardization principles endure but LLMs can now perform fuzzy matching and entity resolution that previously required manual cleanup

extracts.extract: Be sure to troll through your data and look for anomalies. Use the Filter feature or run a Pivot Table to look for problem records.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Trolling through data for anomalies is an enduring quality assurance practice regardless of tools used

extracts.extract: I find it helps to envision your answer before trying to create it.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Envisioning the answer before building the analysis is an enduring analytical thinking practice

extracts.extract: Sometimes leaving the formulas there gives you a sort of "paper trail" of what you did, or allows others to see what you did.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Preserving formulas as a paper trail for reproducibility is an enduring transparency principle
